title: Primary metrics (spend, purchases, CPA, ROAS) decide what works; secondary metrics explain why
insight: Split your ads manager metrics into two categories: primary metrics (amount spent, purchases, cost per purchase, ROAS) that tell you what is working, and secondary/storytelling metrics (frequency, CPM, hook rate, hold rate, CTR) that explain why. Only optimize based on primary metrics — increase budgets, kill campaigns, or scale based on CPA and ROAS. Never make optimization decisions based on secondary metrics alone. A low CTR or high CPM doesn't mean an ad is failing if the CPA is profitable. Secondary metrics help you form hypotheses about creative performance, not make budget decisions.
raw_excerpt: Your primary metrics are going to tell you what is working and these are the only metrics that you should be using to optimize your account... your secondary metrics are going to help illuminate potentially why something is working.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Set up ads manager columns with primary metrics first (spend, purchases, CPA, ROAS), then secondary metrics
Sort by highest spend first to prioritize what's getting delivery
Only make budget/scaling decisions based on primary metrics — use secondary metrics for creative briefs and hypotheses

title: Use unique outbound CTR as the purest click metric — regular CTR includes non-landing-page clicks
insight: Standard click-through rate in Meta ads manager includes clicks that go nowhere, clicks to your profile, and clicks that never reach your landing page. Unique outbound CTR is the purest metric because it counts only individual users who actually clicked through to your landing page. This distinction matters even more after Meta's March 2026 attribution update, which redefines click-through attribution to exclusively count link clicks (shares, saves, and other interactions are reclassified as 'engage-through attribution'). Note: there is no CPC metric for unique outbound clicks specifically, so use cost per link click as the next best proxy.
raw_excerpt: I like looking at unique outbound because this is the one that actually looks at how many individual users are clicking through and actually going through to your landing page. If you look at just the average click-through metric you are actually looking at people who click on your ad go nowhere, click on your ad and end up going to the profile.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Replace standard CTR with unique outbound CTR in your ads manager column setup
Use cost per link click as your CPC proxy since there's no unique outbound CPC metric
After Meta's March 2026 update, review how your click attribution numbers shift as non-link interactions move to engage-through attribution

title: Frequency above 5 over 7 days signals audience saturation — creatives are hitting the same people
insight: When frequency exceeds 5 over the last 7 days or few weeks, it's a warning sign that your campaign is retargeting the same audience repeatedly rather than reaching new people. This suggests your creatives are targeting the same users over and over, or your campaign has exhausted its reachable audience. Monitor frequency alongside reach and impressions: reach shows individual people who saw your ad, impressions shows total times ads were shown (including repeats). High frequency with stagnant reach means you need fresh creative or broader targeting.
raw_excerpt: I actually tend to get a little worried if I see frequency numbers above five across the last 7 days or the last few weeks cuz this might tell me that we are starting to hit a lot of retargeting audiences.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Add frequency to your ads manager dashboard and monitor weekly
If frequency exceeds 5 over 7 days, introduce new creative or broaden targeting
Cross-reference frequency with reach trends — flat reach + rising frequency = audience exhaustion

title: Meta's breakdown effect: algorithm won't scale 'more profitable' ads because performance would degrade at higher spend
insight: A common question is why Meta doesn't push more spend to ads with better CPA or ROAS within a campaign. Meta calls this the 'breakdown effect' — the algorithm has determined that scaling those specific ads further would degrade their performance. Meta has written about this multiple times. Testing it by manually throttling spend to these 'more profitable' ads typically confirms Meta's judgment: they don't perform as well at higher spend. Instead of trying to force spend to low-spend winners, analyze what makes them work creatively and apply those learnings to new ads that can scale.
raw_excerpt: Meta has determined through its algorithm that if it would push more spend to these specific ads that it actually wouldn't be as profitable... I've tested it multiple times for myself in the past by trying to throttle more spend to these ad creatives and typically they don't perform as well.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Don't try to force spend to low-spend 'profitable' ads — trust the algorithm's spend allocation
Instead, analyze the creative elements of those ads and apply learnings to new creatives
Focus optimization efforts on the ads that are actually getting spend from the algorithm

title: ASC campaigns need minimum 20 creatives for optimal algorithm performance
insight: Advantage Shopping Campaigns (ASC) perform significantly better when loaded with at least 20 ad creatives. This minimum gives the algorithm enough variety to find the right creative-audience combinations and avoid the breakdown effect. With fewer creatives, ASC campaigns tend to concentrate spend on one or two ads and exhaust their potential faster. This aligns with the broader creative diversification requirements of Meta's Andromeda system. Start with at least $100/day budget for ASC campaigns.
raw_excerpt: I've seen a lot more success with ASC campaigns in the recent months if you have a lot more ad creative inside of them. I've actually heard that the minimum number you should have in an ASC campaign for optimal performance is 20.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Load ASC campaigns with at least 20 creatives before expecting optimal performance
Start ASC campaigns with a minimum $100/day budget
Regularly add new creatives to maintain the 20+ threshold as older ones fatigue

title: Problem-aware messaging scales longer than bottom-funnel value messaging
insight: In creative testing, bottom-funnel messaging (value-based, competitor comparisons, numbers-heavy) tends to convert quickly by capturing low-hanging fruit but exhausts faster. Problem-aware messaging that speaks to a broader audience — using emotional triggers, questions, and negative marketing hooks — scales longer because it appeals to a wider segment of the funnel (problem-aware and unaware audiences). When analyzing winning creatives, assess where in the marketing journey the messaging targets. Creatives that win long-term tend to have the most scalable messaging that resonates beyond just ready-to-buy customers.
raw_excerpt: Messaging B had the most scalable messaging because it was able to appeal to the widest range of people to more of that problem aware / unaware segment... A was the one that came out of the gate and was starting to convert a lot more quickly because it was getting more of that low hanging fruit... But ultimately the one that ended up running and winning for us the longest was the one that had that more scalable messaging.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Tag each creative with its funnel stage (unaware, problem-aware, solution-aware, product-aware)
Don't declare a winner too early — bottom-funnel creatives convert fast but exhaust quickly
For scaling, prioritize creatives with problem-aware or unaware messaging that can reach broader audiences

title: Qualitative creative analysis: format, creator, messaging angle, and setting all affect performance
insight: Quantitative metrics alone don't explain why a creative wins. A thorough creative analysis should include qualitative dimensions: (1) Format — is it UGC, testimonial, educational, product demo? (2) Creator — does this specific person drive results across multiple creatives? (3) Messaging — what angle, trigger words, and funnel stage does it target? (4) Imagery/setting — production quality and physical location (e.g., car vs. home vs. outdoors) can measurably impact performance. This framework is especially valuable for unicorn creatives that are scaling — pull apart every element so you can replicate the winning formula.
raw_excerpt: The real heart of why a specific ad creative performed or didn't perform that type of insight generally isn't in the data itself it's going to be in the ad creative... the messaging in the type of visuals and even the format that you're using.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: For every winning creative, document: format, creator, messaging angle, setting/imagery, and production style
Track creator performance across multiple creatives to identify talent worth reinvesting in
Use breakdown data (age, placement, gender) alongside qualitative analysis for a complete picture

title: Analyze first, optimize second — separate the two activities to avoid reactive decisions
insight: A common mistake is trying to analyze and optimize simultaneously, which leads to reactive decisions. Separate the two: first, analyze what is working (which campaigns, ads, and creatives are performing) and why (what creative elements, messaging, and audience signals explain the performance). Only after completing the analysis should you take optimization actions like adjusting budgets, pausing ads, or scaling. This prevents knee-jerk reactions to secondary metrics and keeps optimization grounded in a complete understanding of account performance.
raw_excerpt: A mistake I often see is that people try to optimize and analyze at the same time but really the analysis needs to be done first before you start tweaking things. So take a deep breath and focus on one thing at a time.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: Block separate time for analysis (reading data) and optimization (making changes) — never mix them
During analysis, answer two questions only: what is working, and why is it working
Only take action (budget changes, pausing, scaling) after completing your full analysis

title: First-party attribution tools (Triple Whale, Northbeam) only needed at scale with multi-channel spend
insight: Tools like Triple Whale and Northbeam are valuable only when you're managing larger budgets across multiple attribution channels (TV, podcast, influencer, Google, Meta). If you're primarily using Meta for customer acquisition, ads manager alone is sufficient for optimization decisions. These tools don't magically unlock creative-level insights — their value is in Precision attribution across multiple data sources. Master ads manager first, then layer in first-party tools when you scale budgets and diversify channels. For most brands starting out, these tools are an unnecessary expense.
raw_excerpt: First party data tools like triple whale and northbeam are really only good for Brands who are starting to scale up quite a bit but to be honest many Brands don't need them at all... ads manager is going to be sufficient enough for you.
source_title: Motion - How to Analyze Facebook Ads Data the Right Way (The 2026 Guide)
actionable_steps: If you're only running Meta ads, stick with ads manager — don't invest in 3P attribution tools yet
Consider Triple Whale or Northbeam only when you're spending across Meta + Google + TV/podcast/influencer
Master ads manager analysis and column setup before layering in additional tools
